title: Last Minute Biology EOC Cram Session // 25min Crash Bio Review!
body:
Heres the AI version of my message😭

I took my Florida EOC today and scored a 5. There were 51 questions on my exam. Here's an overview of the topics covered:

Few questions on Embryology
Many questions on ecology (environment and ecosystems)
1 or 2 questions on energy in ecosystems/energy pyramid
3 or 4 questions on organelles
1 or 2 questions on parts of the brain
1 question on viscosity of blood
Many questions on cells & cell theory
Few questions on cellular respiration and photosynthesis
2 or 3 questions on monomers, polymers & macromolecules (like lipids, amino acids, monosaccharides, carbohydrates, proteins, glucose, starch)
1 question on identifying a macromolecule, monomer, or polymer based on an image
Many questions on evolution
1 question on Punnett squares
2 or 3 questions on genetics & inheritance (other than Punnett square question)
1 or 2 questions on Taxonomy, classification, & domains
1 or 2 questions on codons in relation to DNA & RNA
2 or 3 questions on natural selection & other biological processes
1 question on a food web with a diagram
3 or 4 questions on understanding scientific theories and laws
1 question on microscopes (specifically viewing cells via microscopes)
2 or 3 questions on the carbon cycle & sustainability
Few questions on plants (reproduction, genetics, etc)
1 question on properties of water
Some questions were common sense. It's important to eliminate answers you know are wrong and to read the entire question and answers carefully. Words like "most likely" or "best" in relation to the answer choices may indicate that the answer is not 100% correct but is the most plausible based on scientific principles.

When answering questions, even if you feel like you totally understand it, find key words and define them.

- Mitosis, for example, is a type of cell division that creates body cells in animals (including humans) and is involved in growth, tissue repair, and asexual reproduction in some organisms. It creates two identical diploid cells with the exact same DNA as the parent cell. During mitosis, cells spend most of their time preparing for division and less time actually dividing. The process involves the stages of prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase, followed by cytokinesis;

- Fungi are heterotrophic organisms that have cytoplasm, cell walls, and are eukaryotic. They are decomposers, breaking down organic matter;

- Bacteria can be autotrophic or heterotrophic and most have cell walls. They are prokaryotic, unicellular organisms with ribosomes. Mutations in bacteria can help them develop immunity/resistance against antibiotics, similar to how animals evolve through evolution.

It's important to understand the relationships between concepts in the questions and how to interpret diagrams based on that. A testing strategy that helped me was to eliminate choices I knew were wrong and select the best answer, flagging the question to come back to it later. Going back and double-checking can help clarify any questions you struggled with. MOST IMPORTANTLY READ THE QUESTIONS AND ANSWERS CAEFULLY TO AVOID OVERLOOKING IMPORTANT INFORMATION OR WORDS THAT SWIFT THE MEANING OF THE STATEMENT!

I hope this helps! Good luck to everyone taking the exam.❤

title: Is Life Better in the USA or Canada? (An Honest Review)
body:
I understand some viewers expected a definitive conclusion given the video’s title, and that’s a fair point. If you came to this video expecting a clear verdict, I can see how the lack of one might feel frustrating. I’ll keep that in mind for future videos.

This video was never meant to hand out a neat, one-size-fits-all answer to a nuanced and deeply personal question.

If I had said, ‘Canada is better’ or ‘the USA is better,’ I’d be oversimplifying something that depends entirely on who you are, what you value, and what your life circumstances look like. That’s why I laid out all the factors—income, cost of living, healthcare, career opportunities, social benefits, and livability—and gave you the tools to weigh them against your own priorities.

Let’s be real—if I claimed that one country is definitively ‘better,’ a lot of people would have (rightfully) called me out for ignoring the nuanced and subjective nature of the topic.

Instead, I made the conscious choice to present an even-handed, data-driven analysis while also sharing my own experiences and the experiences of my guests. My goal was to help you reflect on what’s “better” for you based on what you’re optimizing for.

As for my honest take, I did share it. I said Canada feels safer and offers a better social safety net, while the USA provides unparalleled opportunities for career advancement, which align with my goals right now. That is my honest answer, and that’s why I’m here in the US.

At the end of the day, my goal is to encourage critical thinking, deep conversation, and reflection, not to hand out easy answers. I respect that you might feel differently.

Thanks for watching, and I appreciate everyone who engages thoughtfully in this conversation.

title: Is Life Better in the USA or Canada? (An Honest Review)
body:
Really enjoyed the documentary — I think it’s very precise overall and it sparked a great discussion.

I’m Canadian, but I’ve spent a lot of time in the United States throughout my life, so I’ve seen both systems up close. I’m also an entrepreneur and commercial real estate investor, which I think materially changes this comparison.

One important piece that often gets missed is that entrepreneurs in Canada can access tax strategies that are extremely similar to those in the U.S. With proper structuring and good accounting, the gap most people assume exists simply isn’t there. For most entrepreneurs, real estate is part of the portfolio — and Canada has millions of renters and millions of development and commercial opportunities.

From an entrepreneurial standpoint, the ceiling here is just as high. You can absolutely build generational wealth in Canada — and many Canadian families already have.

Another point you mentioned at the end about opportunity and like-minded creative circles in the U.S.: being Canadian doesn’t mean being confined to Canada. For many of us, Canada is the home base, not the limit. We travel, network, and do business globally — including New York, LA, and beyond.

Canada also has one of the strongest passports in the world, allowing visa-free or visa-on-arrival access to most countries, which gives entrepreneurs and creatives enormous flexibility. You can spend significant time abroad and still maintain residency and healthcare coverage.

Speaking of healthcare — that’s a major structural difference. Entrepreneurs in Canada don’t have to budget for private healthcare, and now there’s even new publicly funded dental coverage that covers a portion of dental costs.

I think when you factor in entrepreneurship, real estate, mobility, and systems — the comparison looks very different.

Thanks again for the documentary — really well done.

title: Is Life Better in the USA or Canada? (An Honest Review)
body: I've spent most of my life living near Vancouver. I lived in Berlin Germany for a few years for work and the lifestyle and cost of living was FAR better. I was making about $4000/month CDN. I had my own nice apartment right downtown, grocery stores were less than a minute walk away, I could afford to eat out at restaurants every day for lunch, I lived very comfortably and I was putting lots of money into savings as well. Then I moved back to the Vancouver area in Canada, I now make around $6000/month CDN, which after tax is actually closer to $3000. I could not afford to live on my own, had to live with my mom in a small townhouse, and after all bills and necessities were covered, I had around $300 to cover groceries, which $300 really doesnt get much. No money yo put away. So my mom and I decided to sell our small townhouse and moved to the middle of nowhere in Northern Alberta on nearly 200 acres. Now I can afford bills, groceries, and have money left over, and I have privacy and lots of land. My mom and I have our own houses on the acreage. I love it, never going back to the city life.

title: Is Life Better in the USA or Canada? (An Honest Review)
body:
This is great video! Thank you. It really sheds a lot of light on how things are in the US and other parts of Canada other than Quebec.

Only thing I would add to that is that when Quebec residents travel to another province (Ontario in my case), you will not get free at face medical care. It's not nationwide for us. You will have to pay up or you will be refused. Mind you, if you have company insurance you'll be fine. I did not when I went even though that shouldn't have been a worry of mine. They say we need to pay up because it takes too long for the administration to get the money back from Quebec. I was refused at a clinic and Saint-Michaels Hospital in the middle of Anaphylaxis. I could of died and not one attendant cared. Not one even looked up from their computers to make eye contact with me the moment they saw my medicare card from Quebec. One Government Representative at Ontario Works in the Jane/Finch of Toronto said to me, "I thought you guys separated already" and I was given a dirty look.

As well, it has been nearly impossible to get assistance after my accident here in Quebec. As far as I'm concerned as a Quebec resident, medicare has been useless. I'm at a point that I may get insurance and go to private clinics because of how flaud the medicare system is here. One yr I was asked to pay $1485 to Quebec Medicare because I was supposed to pay monthly for it. Never in my 45 yrs of living here did I ever have to pay. That's what our taxes are for?! Very Odd! Something is definitely wrong here as a resident. Especially an Anglo. You should do more research on Quebec. Don't look at it as A tourist. As a resident, I sometimes don't even feel like I'm living in Canada. The way you describe the US is how I feel here. I go elsewhere in Canada without private insurance with my medical condition I'd be screwed if anything happened.

I had a friend who had a tumor the size of a grapefruit on her uterus and had been on a waiting list for 2 yrs as it was growing and growing. She had to get insurance to attend a private clinic otherwise she'd be dead today. Another friend of mine had to wait until he was in Stage 4 cancer before he got apprroved for radiation treatement. He has since passed away.

Right now, I'm looking into leaving Quebec and researching the best place to move to. So far, it's Edmonton.

Once I'm settled I will be going public about my horrible life experience here in this province. People need to know the truth.
author: @MadamGato7

title: Truth About Living in Texas No One Talks About
body: The snowfall wasn't what caused the power outage in 2021. It was the temperatures getting down into the single digits for multiple days. Colder areas are better prepared for this, but in Texas most people have either natural gas or electric heating, primarily heat pumps. The problem with heat pumps, and why they're not relied on heavily in colder areas, is that they're only effective down to about 20ºF. Below that, they switch into "emergency heating" which is a form of resistance heating and only about 20-25% as efficient as a heat pump. So the grid saw a massive spike in energy usage in a small timeframe. Something that doesn't happen in colder areas where people would never consider using electrical resistance heating as it's too expensive.

title: What THEY'RE NOT TELLING YOU About Moving To Florida | Honest Review 5 Years Later
body:
We moved down last July from NJ. We're in Polk County, right next door to Tampa. The heat did take some getting used to, but it can be done. What I loved is that we never turned on the heat this past winter. My son wore pants to school one day the entire year. It was shorts the rest of the time. But not having to wear even a light jacket in the winter, while my co-workers were shoveling snow makes up for a little heat. There have been many days so far this summer where it's been hotter back up there than down here.

Traffic lights - whoever set up these lights needs to be tarred and feathered. And there's very little straight line connection, you need to make lots of turns on the grid and sit through multiple lights.

Roads - So much better than NJ, but just this past weekend, my wife managed to find the 1 pothole, and cracked two rims and blew both tires. It was filled in the next day.

Bugs - one trick I brought with me was giving the dragonflies a place to sit, right next to our deck. Dragonflies eat many times their body weight in smaller bugs. We live next to a fairly sizeable pond, but the bamboo stakes we put in the ground near the house attracted plenty of dragonflies, and as a result we had only 1 mosquito bit all last summer. Our neighbors put them up, as well, and our neighborhood has been pretty much bug free.

Water - yep, it sucks. 'cold' water is 80+ degrees in summer. We ended up getting an extra ice machine to keep up with family demand. We also have 2 high capacity brita filters which work very well.

Schools - not touched on in this video, but Florida has a very open School Choice program. They also have specialized academies within the schools, starting in middle school. Our son is in the aerospace, engineering and robotics academy at his middle school. There are programs for pretty much everything. If college isn't something your kid wants, they can do one of the trade academies, and in most cases graduate with the certifications they need to start working.

Floods - we're inland a bit, so floods aren't an issue for the house, but the streets do sometimes get temporarily blocked after a few downpours.

We had done a scouting trip the previous year to look for good neighborhoods, and to do things like compare grocery and gas prices. Groceries, compared to NJ, were similar. Some things were more expensive, some less, but the average grocery bill was only a couple dollars less. Gas was about 25 cents more a gallon, but I've seen them swing more than 50 cents a gallon each way in only a matter of weeks down here.

title: Day In The Life of The #1 BBQ In Texas
body:
Over the course of 2 months of editing, I realized a lot of the video games, anime, movies, and soundtracks I listened to during that time started to influence my video style, especially in the music selection. Can’t remember the amount of times I fell asleep in my chair while searching for the right track, but it was a lot, haha.

A few of inspirations: Your Lie in April, Xenoblade Chronicles, RL Grime, The Bear, Chef (Jon Favreau), Lofi Girl, and Ocean’s Eleven.

Sometimes I’d finish editing for the night, start playing video games, then immediately rush back to editing because of a boss fight, a cutscene, or a musical theme that inspired me to change up a particular song for a scene.

sounds kinda weird, but man I love video games and anime

title: Day In The Life of The #1 BBQ In Texas
body: In September 2022 hurricane Ian hit my town (Fort Myers, FL) and we evacuated to my aunts house a cpl hrs away. While we were there me n my Dad came across this documentary and we both really loved the story. My dad loves good bbq restaurants and has traveled to a lot of different ones all over our state . The next month October 2022 while our town was rebuilding, i got a new job with a $3000 sign-on bonus. I told my mom for Xmas, as one of my dads gifts, I'm using that bonus money to fly me n my dad to Goldees bbq in March for his birthday. One month later on Nov 22, 2022 my father died unexpectedly. It crushed me. But I still took the trip in March 2023 to honor his memory n celebrate his birthday. I brought one of my best friends who my dad really liked in his place. We got there at 6am and we were number 9 n 10 in line. Best bbq I've ever had!  It's painful to watch this video but it also reminds me of my friend who got to experience waiting in the cold for 5 hours for BBQ with me lol. This year, on the same day from last year, me and that same friend went to one of our local barbecue restaurants we like here and had lunch in his memory and in memory of the trip we took. I love n miss you Dad and I'm sorry you didn't get to experience the #1 BBQ in Texas. Thank you Goldees and Alvin for making this unforgettable documentary ❤️🙏🏻

title: Picture Yourself at Texas State
body: You do remember that Roger Williams was a Puritan like Puritans on The Mayflower also called, "Separatists" after The Reformation of Denominations? (Reformation / Britannica). Where do you think the term 'Separatist" vs "Loyalist" came from? Hmmmm? Baptists, Amish, Puritans, Lutherans, Ana-Baptists, along with many other church denominations falls under Protestantism (Protestantism / Britannica). They mention in Britannica that Protestants were against The Church of England, but they fail to mention that Protestants were also against Catholics as well. Martin Luther, a Protestant, who formed Lutheranism also came out with The New Testament in German. William Tyndale, another Protestant Leader, was the first to publish The New Testament into English. The Bishop at that time didn't like his translation, so William was later burned at the stake just like Edward Wightman (Encyclopedia). Edward Wightman was an Ana-Baptist and the last man burned at the stake in England by King James for heresy. Britannica does mention him as the reason why Capital Punishment came to America under "Burning At The Stake." Ya know....Roger Williams came out with The Separation of Church & State that transcended down into our 1st Amendment through Thomas Jefferson (Library of Congress - "A Wall of Separation" Danbury, CT) to grant religious freedom which was to prevent a person from becoming persecuted for their religious freedom to speak their beliefs, so that a person wouldn't be hung or burned at the stake for their beliefs. It was also to prevent 'governing powers' from trying to influence "scripture." The Baptist Church was to be simple. A Pastor, Senior Pastor, and perhaps a Reverend. "No hierarchy" like The Roman Catholic Church. If you read deeper into The Reformation, you'd understand that. It was King Henry The VIII that founded The Church of England and Protestantism, but Protestants later turned against The Church of England. Also, through history and Britannica, there were past battles between Protestants and Catholics between The 1500s and The American Revolution. Look it up.

title: Texas State University Campus Tour
body: It's hard to describe how much different the campus is now compared to how it was back in 1965 to 1973.  Originally it was called "Southwest Texas State Teachers College" and was referred to as "the friendly college on the hill".  The name changed (in the 1950s I think) to "Southwest Texas State College" ... That's what it was called when I started there in the summer of 65.  And it was still somewhat like the friendly college on the hill at that time (though there were former students that would disagree I think).  But things started changing fairly quickly about that time (due to LBJ's presidency) ... it started growing fast (into becoming what seems to me to be a shrine to LBJ).  I think it was some time in 1966 that the name changed again ... this time to "Southwest Texas State University" (I remember at the time I wasn't especially crazy about that myself ... changes were coming pretty quickly).  Anyway, after a Bachelor's and Master's degree I left for College Station.  And since that time it's grown into what seems to me to be a monolithic concrete jungle (especially with that walkway that goes over a good part of the campus to connect it to what used to be the San Marcos Academy which somewhere along the line became part of the University campus after the Academy went belly up).  That walkway literally changed the whole feeling of the campus ... and there's so many students now.  Back in 65 I think the student population was around 5 to 6 thousand.  A far cry from what it is today.  I lived in a number of places off campus back then.  Though I suspect they're not around anymore ... except maybe for 202 Walnut ... it might still be there.   And of course finally the name was finally changed to Texas State University.   I guess time marches on.  Anyway this was a nice tour.
